name: Luxembourg
summary: Tiny ultra-high-income tech hub with a near-free English MSc, top net pay, free spouse work and a 5-year dual-citizenship route; biggest levers are tax/healthcare/safety, biggest drags are a tiny job market that may not absorb both engineers, New-Delhi-routed visas amid a 54.9% BD Schengen refusal rate, brutal housing costs, and a Luxembourgish-language citizenship test.

links.title: Long-term National Visa - Embassy of Luxembourg in New Delhi (official)

job-market.summary: High-paying ICT/fintech hub: SW eng avg ~€72k, 75th pct ~€88k; but a ~680k market may not absorb two senior engineers.
job-market.reasoning: Luxembourg is a wealthy ICT/fintech/EU-institutions hub with high software-engineer pay (Glassdoor 2026 average ~€72k, 25th-75th pct ~€59k-88k) and a market that is unusually tight at the senior tier, with fintech demand lifted by MiCA and EU AI Act compliance and tech roles ~22% of professional listings. English works widely in the international finance/tech scene. The decisive caveat for this couple is scale: ~680,000 residents plus heavy FR/BE/DE cross-border competition mean the absolute number of senior SW/AI openings is limited, so placing BOTH simultaneously is less certain than in Germany or the Netherlands, holding the score in the good-with-caveats band.
evidence: Glassdoor 2026: SW engineer avg ~€72,250; 25th-75th pct ~€59k-88k
Tech roles ~22% of professional listings; senior availability the key constraint (2026)
Fintech/data demand lifted by MiCA and EU AI Act compliance; rates up 6-9% 2024-25
Tiny labour market (~680k residents) caps absolute number of senior roles
Heavy FR/BE/DE cross-border commuter competition for the same jobs

visa-access.summary: No Dhaka decision: long-stay D visas routed via New Delhi, 3-5 month process; BD Schengen refusal hit 54.9% in 2024.
visa-access.reasoning: There is no Luxembourg mission in Dhaka, so Bangladesh residents apply through the Embassy in New Delhi: first a temporary authorisation to stay (Ministry replies within ~60 days, valid 90 days), then an in-person D-visa application in New Delhi, with the whole process running 3-5 months. Bangladesh's overall Schengen refusal rate rose sharply to 54.9% in 2024 (from 43.3% in 2023), signalling heightened scrutiny of BD applicants generally, though long-stay study/work D visas backed by a university place and proof of funds are a distinct, more favourable channel than tourist visas. The New-Delhi routing, courier/biometrics friction, document scrutiny and elevated refusal climate place this in the mixed-with-meaningful-friction band.
evidence: MAEE New Delhi: long-stay D visas for Bangladesh residents processed in New Delhi, not Dhaka
Temporary authorisation to stay first (~60 days), then in-person D visa in New Delhi
End-to-end long-stay process ~3-5 months (Atlys 2025)
Bangladesh Schengen refusal rate 54.9% in 2024, up from 43.3% in 2023
Luxembourg's overall 2024 Schengen refusal rate ~12.6% (lower than many peers)

links.title: Long-term National Visa - Embassy of Luxembourg in New Delhi (official)

citizenship.summary: 5 years residence + dual citizenship since 2009; passport rank ~4th (185-188 visa-free); but Luxembourgish Sproochentest required.
citizenship.reasoning: Naturalisation requires 5 years' legal residence (last 12 months uninterrupted), and Luxembourg has allowed dual/multiple nationality since 1 January 2009, so the couple need not renounce Bangladeshi citizenship - directly matching their preference. The resulting passport is among the world's strongest (Henley 2025-26 rank ~4th, ~185-188 visa-free destinations). The friction is the Sproochentest in Luxembourgish (A2 speaking / B1 listening, €75) plus the 24-hour 'Vivre ensemble' civics course; Luxembourgish is a niche language with little day-to-day exposure for English-speaking tech workers, making the language bar the main drag and holding the score mid-band despite the fast clock.
evidence: 5 years' residence, last 12 months uninterrupted (Guichet.lu/justarrived.lu)
Dual nationality allowed since 1 Jan 2009 - no renunciation for Bangladeshis
Sproochentest: A2 speaking + B1 listening in Luxembourgish, €75 fee
24-hour 'Vivre ensemble' civics course (or exam) also required
Passport rank ~4th (Henley 2025-26), ~185-188 visa-free destinations
8,223 acquired Luxembourgish nationality in 2025 (15.7% via naturalisation)

links.title: Luxembourg passport ranking 2026 / visa-free (Henley Passport Index)

post-study-work.summary: 12-month job-search/business permit for non-EU Master's grads; not renewable, must switch to a worker permit before expiry.
post-study-work.reasoning: Non-EU graduates with a Luxembourg Master's degree (or a defended PhD thesis) get a residence permit to look for a job or create a business, valid 12 months per the official Guichet.lu page, with a simplified switch to an employee permit once a job is found. It is not renewable: holders must transition to an employee/self-employed permit before it lapses and prove monthly resources of >=80% of the social-inclusion income (€80 issuance fee, apply >=30 days before the student permit expires). Solid and clear, but shorter than the 3-year open-work top anchor and limited to Master's/PhD graduates, so it sits in the good band.
evidence: Official Guichet.lu: 12-month 'job search or business creation' permit, not renewable
Eligibility: passed a Master's diploma or defended a research PhD thesis in Luxembourg
Simplified procedure to switch to employee permit once a job offer is secured
Apply >=30 days before student permit expires; €80 issuance fee
Financial proof during search: >=80% of social-inclusion income

links.title: Staying in Luxembourg to find a job after studies (Guichet.lu, official)
EU Blue Card / highly qualified worker permit (Guichet.lu, official)

spouse-family.summary: Family members get free labour-market access on arrival since the 7 Aug 2023 law; strongest in the work phase.
spouse-family.reasoning: Under the law of 7 August 2023, any third-country family member holding a 'family member' residence permit on the basis of family reunification has free access to the labour market as soon as they arrive - no separate work permit needed - which is ideal for a reunited spouse. As two engineers, both also have strong standalone routes (student, then Blue Card/employee). The weaker point is the study phase: a student can sponsor a spouse only with a >=1-year permit and proof of resources for both, and student-stage reunification is more discretionary, so the couple may prefer to enter on parallel independent permits or time reunification to the work phase.
evidence: Law of 7 Aug 2023: family-member permit holders work freely on arrival, no work permit
All University of Luxembourg students may work during studies regardless of nationality
Both partners can hold independent student / Blue Card / employee permits
Student-sponsor reunification needs a >=1-year permit + resources for both
Children's schooling: free multilingual public system plus international schools

links.title: Salaried work for a third-country family member (Guichet.lu, official)

msc-study.summary: UL MSc Information & Computer Sciences: English-taught, ~€400/year, 2 years; students may work 15 hrs/week in term.
msc-study.reasoning: The University of Luxembourg's Master in Information and Computer Sciences is taught entirely in English at roughly €200/semester (~€400/year) over 2 years/4 semesters - near-free for a top-young research university - and all enrolled students may work part-time (15 hrs/week in term, 40 hrs/week in holidays). A spouse can accompany. The catalogue is small (essentially one main public university) versus Germany's dozens, and Luxembourg City living costs are very high, which caps the score below the abundant-and-cheap top tier but keeps it clearly favourable for this IT couple.
evidence: UL MSc Information & Computer Sciences: English, ~€200/semester (~€400/yr), 2 years
Most UL Master's €0-5,200/yr; this programme at the low end of the scale
Students may work 15 hrs/week during term, up to 40 hrs/week in holidays
Single main public university - limited breadth vs larger EU systems
High Luxembourg City living costs (~€1,200-2,100/month) offset low tuition

links.title: Master in Information and Computer Sciences (University of Luxembourg, official)
Working as a student - University of Luxembourg (official)

pr-pathway.summary: EU long-term resident status after 5 years' continuous legal residence; since 1 Jul 2024 study/EU time can count toward the clock.
pr-pathway.reasoning: Long-term resident (EU) status is available after 5 years' uninterrupted legal residence (Guichet.lu). Since 1 July 2024, time spent as a student or researcher (including in another EU state) counts toward the 5 years, which directly helps a study-then-work path. Blue Card holders can reach it via 5 years' EU residence with the last 2 in Luxembourg. The route is residence-duration based with no points test, so it is predictable, but the standard ~5-year timeline is not fast and continuity rules apply (absences capped at 6 consecutive / 10 cumulative months over the 5 years).
evidence: EU long-term resident status after 5 years' uninterrupted legal residence (Guichet.lu)
Since 1 Jul 2024 student/researcher time (incl. other EU states) counts toward the 5 years
Blue Card route: 5 years' EU residence, last 2 in Luxembourg
Absence limits: no more than 6 consecutive / 10 cumulative months over 5 years
No points test - residence-duration based and predictable

links.title: Long-term resident status for third-country nationals (Guichet.lu, official)
Luxembourg: Relaxed EU Long-term Residency Calculation Rules (Fragomen)

income-cost.summary: Top European net pay (low tax wedge) but Europe's costliest housing: city avg ~€35.6/m2, 2-bed ~€2,900/month.
income-cost.reasoning: Net pay is among Europe's best - a married dual-earner couple faces a low tax wedge (OECD 2025 married-with-children wedge 20.7%, net rate ~9.9%) on SW salaries of ~€59k-88k. The offset is severe housing: Luxembourg City asking rents averaged ~€35.6/m2 in Feb 2026 (up 4.3% y/y), a 2-bed apartment ~€2,900/month and Kirchberg over €48/m2, with purchase prices ~€8,300/m2. Two engineering incomes still allow real savings, but rent is a major and persistent drag, pulling the score to the low end of the good band.
evidence: OECD 2025: married-with-children tax wedge 20.7%; employee net rate ~9.9%
Luxembourg City asking rent avg ~€35.6/m2 (Feb 2026), up 4.31% y/y
2-bed apartment ~€2,900/month; Kirchberg >€48/m2 (most expensive)
Apartment purchase ~€8,322/m2; avg apartment ~€725k (Q3 2025)
Forecast rent growth 1.5-3% in 2026 amid chronic undersupply

healthcare.summary: Universal CNS insurance covers ~99% of residents; high quality with 80-100% reimbursement, costs below EU average.
healthcare.reasoning: Luxembourg runs a statutory health-insurance model under the CNS covering ~99% of residents, reimbursing 80-100% of medical costs with high-quality, easy-to-navigate care and strong welfare/family benefits. CCSS affiliation is mandatory for workers; students not covered through employment can join voluntarily (~€151/month in 2025, 3-month waiting period). Near the top tier; slightly below 90 because reimbursement is pay-then-claim with some caps (e.g., visit limits, referral/prior-approval rules) and voluntary student membership carries a fee and waiting period.
evidence: CNS statutory insurance covers ~99% of residents (universal system)
Reimbursement 80-100% of medical expenses; pay-then-claim model
Mandatory CCSS affiliation for workers; care widely rated among Europe's best
Voluntary student/non-worker CNS membership ~€151.41/month (2025), 3-month wait
Some caps apply (e.g., visit limits, referral/prior-approval requirements)

culture-language.summary: Highly multilingual, English-friendly daily life (~47% foreign-born); but Luxembourgish (Sproochentest) gates citizenship.
culture-language.reasoning: Luxembourg is one of the most multilingual countries in the world (French, German, Luxembourgish, plus widespread English in international finance/tech), so English-speaking newcomers function well day-to-day and at work, and a roughly 47%-foreign-born, cosmopolitan society is open and high in livability and safety. The constraint is Luxembourgish - a niche language needed for the naturalisation Sproochentest (A2/B1) and parts of officialdom - which is harder to acquire incidentally than French or German. High openness lifts the score; the citizenship language bar holds it mid-band.
evidence: Trilingual official languages (FR/DE/LB) + widespread workplace English
Roughly 47% foreign-born; cosmopolitan, high-livability society
Luxembourgish (Sproochentest A2/B1) required for naturalisation
English works across international finance/tech and much of daily life
Luxembourgish has limited everyday exposure for English speakers

safety-law.summary: WJP Rule of Law 2025: 7th of 143 (0.83); low crime, strong courts and protections for residents.
safety-law.reasoning: Luxembourg ranks 7th of 143 in the WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 with an overall score of 0.83 (up slightly y/y), reflecting strong constraints on government, low corruption, order and security, and fair civil/criminal justice. Crime is low and the legal framework (incl. the Centre for Equal Treatment) protects residents. A top-tier rule-of-law environment; tempered only slightly by reported discrimination experiences among minorities (see Muslim-diaspora).
evidence: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025: 7th of 143, overall score 0.83
Score rose <1% y/y; strong on order & security and constraints on government
Low crime and corruption; independent judiciary
Centre for Equal Treatment (CET) handles discrimination complaints
EU member with robust civil-rights protections

links.title: WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 - Luxembourg country page
WJP Rule of Law Index 2025 (full report PDF)

politics.summary: Stable CSV-DP coalition (since Nov 2023, 41/60 seats); economy reliant on foreign labour, no dominant far-right (ADR ~8%).
politics.reasoning: The CSV-DP coalition under PM Luc Frieden (formed 17 November 2023, ~41 of 60 seats) provides stable, centre-right consensus government that posted a 2024 budget surplus, and Luxembourg's economy structurally depends on foreign and cross-border workers, keeping skilled-migration routes (Blue Card, student-to-work) intact. The far-right ADR holds only ~5/60 seats (~8%) and a National-Rally-style party is widely judged electorally implausible given the country's foreigner-heavy makeup. The coalition's majority is slim - a modest stability risk - but policy volatility toward skilled migrants is low.
evidence: CSV-DP coalition under PM Luc Frieden since 17 Nov 2023, ~41/60 seats
Economy reliant on foreign/cross-border labour; pro-skilled-migration
Far-right ADR holds only ~5/60 seats (~8%); no dominant far-right force
2024 budget surplus; government emphasises stability
EU Pact on Migration & Asylum implementation underway (Home Affairs)

tax.summary: Low effective burden for a married dual-earner couple: 20.7% wedge, ~90.1% take-home for family with kids (OECD 2025).
tax.reasoning: Luxembourg combines a progressive income-tax scale (0-42%) with joint taxation for married couples, giving one of the OECD's lower effective burdens for families. OECD Taxing Wages 2025 puts the married-with-two-children tax wedge at 20.7% (10th-lowest in the OECD vs 26.2% average) with employee net rate ~9.9% and take-home ~90.1%. A single average worker faces a higher 40.2% wedge and marginal wedges reach ~57.4%, but for this dual-earner married couple, joint filing and family benefits make take-home strong relative to most of Western Europe.
evidence: Income tax 0-42% progressive; spouses taxed jointly (Luxembourg)
OECD 2025: married-with-2-children tax wedge 20.7% (10th-lowest; OECD avg 26.2%)
Employee net rate ~9.9%; take-home ~90.1% of gross for that household
Single average worker wedge 40.2% (15th-highest); marginal wedge ~57.4%
Joint filing materially lowers the married couple's effective burden

muslim-diaspora.summary: ~40-50k Muslims (~6%), 6 mosques, halal in cities; but tiny Bangladeshi community and notable reported discrimination.
muslim-diaspora.reasoning: Islam is the second-largest religion (~40,000-50,000 Muslims, ~6% of residents) with six mosques (Mamer/ICC, Luxembourg City, Esch, Wiltz, Diekirch) and halal food in cities, but the community is mainly North African, Balkan and Syrian - the Bangladeshi/South-Asian presence is very small, so established BD networks are thin and halal options are limited in rural areas. A 2018 law bans face veils in public spaces, and EU FRA findings show roughly half of Luxembourg's Muslims report experiencing racism, a real if not extreme sentiment concern that holds the score mid-band.
evidence: ~40,000-50,000 Muslims (~6%); Islam the second-largest religion (2025)
Six mosques; Grand Mosque + Islamic Cultural Centre in Mamer/Luxembourg City
Community mainly North African/Balkan/Syrian; very small Bangladeshi presence
Halal food available in cities; limited in rural areas, no local halal slaughterhouse
2018 law bans face veils in public; EU FRA: ~50% of Muslims report racism
